results.possibleIdentity:
Robert Parnes, Ph.D. (1931–2020) was a physicist and technical researcher active during the late 1960s and 1970s, known for his work in industrial physics and later in organic agriculture and education. He worked as an astrophysicist at the General Electric (GE) Research Laboratory in New York, where he specialized in fields such as physics, optics, spectroscopy, and light equipment. His technical background in projection and cathode rays was representative of the industrial research conducted at GE during this period, which frequently intersected with the activities of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. E.A.T. indexed engineers and scientists from major laboratories like GE to collaborate with artists on technological projects. 

In the late 1960s, Parnes turned down a promotion at General Electric to transition into the 'back-to-the-land' movement, co-founding a biodynamic farm in Chester, New York, with colleagues of E.E. Pfeiffer. He eventually moved to Maine, where he became a key scientific advisor for the Woods End Agricultural Institute and a prominent figure in the organic farming movement. He authored several definitive texts, including 'Fertile Soil: A Grower's Guide to Organic & Inorganic Fertilizers' (1986/1990). Parnes was also involved in interdisciplinary education at the University of Michigan, where he co-developed the 'Matter, Energy and Life' (MEL) course and created the 'Confer' computer conferencing system in the mid-1970s, applying his technical skills to group communication and scientific literacy.
